heading: (intro)
raw: Directed by Otto Preminger. Written by Wendell Mayes, based on the 1958 novel by Robert Traver.
clean: Directed by Otto Preminger. Written by Wendell Mayes, based on the 1958 novel by Robert Traver.

heading: Paul Bieglerv
raw: As a lawyer, I've had to learn that people aren't just good or just bad. People are many things.
clean: As a lawyer, I've had to learn that people aren't just good or just bad. People are many things.

heading: Paul Bieglerv
raw: The prosecution would like to separate the motive from the act. Well, that's like trying to take the core from an apple without breaking the skin.
clean: The prosecution would like to separate the motive from the act. Well, that's like trying to take the core from an apple without breaking the skin.

heading: Paul Bieglerv
raw: I'm just a humble country lawyer trying to do the best I can against this brilliant prosecutor from the big city of Lansing.
clean: I'm just a humble country lawyer trying to do the best I can against this brilliant prosecutor from the big city of Lansing.

heading: Paul Bieglerv
raw: [after cross-examining a convicted felon] Your Honor, I don't think I can dignify this - -creature - - with any more questions.
clean: Your Honor, I don't think I can dignify this - -creature - - with any more questions.

heading: Paul Bieglerv
raw: Look, Laura, believe me, I don't usually complain of an attractive jiggle, but just you save that jiggle for your husband to look at, if and when I get him out of jail.
clean: Look, Laura, believe me, I don't usually complain of an attractive jiggle, but just you save that jiggle for your husband to look at, if and when I get him out of jail.

heading: Parnell Emmett McCarthy
raw: The lieutenant goes to Quill's place and plugs Mr. Quill about five times, which causes Mr. Quill to promptly die of lead poisoning.
clean: The lieutenant goes to Quill's place and plugs Mr. Quill about five times, which causes Mr. Quill to promptly die of lead poisoning.

heading: Parnell Emmett McCarthy
raw: You know I used to think the world looked better through a glass of whiskey. It doesn't. I think I'll keep it this way. Looks nice.
clean: You know I used to think the world looked better through a glass of whiskey. It doesn't. I think I'll keep it this way. Looks nice.

heading: Judge Weaver
raw: Now, Mr. Dancer, get off the panties. You've done enough damage.
clean: Now, Mr. Dancer, get off the panties. You've done enough damage.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Judge Weaver: Mr. Biegler, you finally got your rape into the case, and I think all the details should now be made clear to the jury. What exactly was the undergarment just referred to?
clean: Mr. Biegler, you finally got your rape into the case, and I think all the details should now be made clear to the jury. What exactly was the undergarment just referred to?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: Panties, Your Honor.
clean: Panties, Your Honor.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Judge Weaver': Do you expect this subject to come up again?
clean: Do you expect this subject to come up again?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: Yes, Sir.
clean: Yes, Sir.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Judge Weaver: There's a certain light connotation attached to the word "panties." Can we find another name for them?
clean: There's a certain light connotation attached to the word "panties." Can we find another name for them?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Mitch Lodwick: I never heard my wife call 'em anything else.
clean: I never heard my wife call 'em anything else.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Judge Weaver: Mr. Biegler?
clean: Mr. Biegler?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: I'm a bachelor, Your Honor.
clean: I'm a bachelor, Your Honor.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Judge Weaver: That's a great help. Mr. Dancer?
clean: That's a great help. Mr. Dancer?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Claude Dancer: When I was overseas during the war, Your Honor, I learned a French word. I'm afraid that might be slightly suggestive.
clean: When I was overseas during the war, Your Honor, I learned a French word. I'm afraid that might be slightly suggestive.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Judge Weaver: Most French words are.
clean: Most French words are.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Lt. Frederick Manion: How can a jury disregard what it's already heard?
clean: How can a jury disregard what it's already heard?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: [shaking head] They can't, Lieutenant. They can't.
clean: They can't, Lieutenant. They can't.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: You're fired.
clean: You're fired.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Maida Rutledge: You can't fire me until you pay me.
clean: You can't fire me until you pay me.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: Mr. Paquette, what would you call a man with an insatiable penchant for women?
clean: Mr. Paquette, what would you call a man with an insatiable penchant for women?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Alphonse Paquette: A what?
clean: A what?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: A penchant... a desire... taste... passion?
clean: A penchant... a desire... taste... passion?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Alphonse Paquette: Well, uh, ladies' man, I guess. Or maybe just a damn fool!
clean: Well, uh, ladies' man, I guess. Or maybe just a damn fool!

heading: Dialogue
raw: Judge Weaver: Just answer the questions, Mr. Paquette. The attorneys will provide the wisecracks.
clean: Just answer the questions, Mr. Paquette. The attorneys will provide the wisecracks.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Lt. Frederick Manion: [Roars at Duke Miller, who has just given his testimony] You're a liar! You're a lousy, stinking liar!
clean: You're a liar! You're a lousy, stinking liar!

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: I apologize to the court for my client's outburst. But it's almost excusable, since the prosecution has seen fit to put a felon on the stand to testify against an officer in the United States Army.
clean: I apologize to the court for my client's outburst. But it's almost excusable, since the prosecution has seen fit to put a felon on the stand to testify against an officer in the United States Army.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Mitch Lodwick: Objection. No evidence has been introduced to make Mrs. Manion's appearance relevant to this case.
clean: Objection. No evidence has been introduced to make Mrs. Manion's appearance relevant to this case.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: Well, no evidence has been introduced to make Barney Quill's appearance relevant' you didn't object to the question then. Is that because you know Barney Quill bathed, changed and cooled off after he raped and beat hell out of this poor woman!
clean: Well, no evidence has been introduced to make Barney Quill's appearance relevant' you didn't object to the question then. Is that because you know Barney Quill bathed, changed and cooled off after he raped and beat hell out of this poor woman!

heading: Dialogue
raw: Mitch Lodwick: Your Honor, everyone in this courtroom is being tried except Frederick Manion! I must protest...
clean: Your Honor, everyone in this courtroom is being tried except Frederick Manion! I must protest...

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: Now listen! This is a cross examination in a murder case, it's not a high school debate! What are you and Dancer trying to do, railroad this soldier into the clink?
clean: Now listen! This is a cross examination in a murder case, it's not a high school debate! What are you and Dancer trying to do, railroad this soldier into the clink?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Parnell Emmett McCarthy: Did you give the lieutenant the Well-Known Lecture?
clean: Did you give the lieutenant the Well-Known Lecture?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: If you mean, did I coach him into a phony story, no.
clean: If you mean, did I coach him into a phony story, no.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Parnell Emmett McCarthy: Maybe you're too pure, Paul. Too pure for the natural impurities of the law.
clean: Maybe you're too pure, Paul. Too pure for the natural impurities of the law.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Parnell Emmett McCarthy: Dear Mr. Biegler, so sorry, but I had to leave suddenly: I was seized by an irresistible impulse. Frederick Manion.
clean: Dear Mr. Biegler, so sorry, but I had to leave suddenly: I was seized by an irresistible impulse. Frederick Manion.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: [chuckles with a wry smile] Now how in the world are we gonna face Maida?
clean: Now how in the world are we gonna face Maida?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Parnell Emmett McCarthy: [Pulling empty bottle out of trash barrel] Gin. I knew there was something wrong with that guy. I never saw a gin drinker yet you could trust.
clean: Gin. I knew there was something wrong with that guy. I never saw a gin drinker yet you could trust.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: Well, partner, what do you say we go and see our first client?
clean: Well, partner, what do you say we go and see our first client?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Parnell Emmett McCarthy: Who might that be?
clean: Who might that be?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: Mary Pilant. We're going to administer Barney Quill's estate.
clean: Mary Pilant. We're going to administer Barney Quill's estate.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Parnell Emmett McCarthy: That's what i call poetic justice for everybody.
clean: That's what i call poetic justice for everybody.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Paul Biegler: Yeah.
clean: Yeah.
